title: Gigantes plaki
subtitle: Greek baked butter beans in tomato
excerpt: Giant white beans baked low and slow in a tomato sauce rich with olive oil, garlic, and dill, until the beans are creamy and the sauce reduced to a thick, flavourful glaze. One of the best things you can make with a tin of beans. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Gigantes plaki is a staple of Greek meze and home cooking, documented by Claudia Roden in The Food of the Eastern Mediterranean and by Diane Kochilas in The Greek Vegetarian. The dish uses the large dried white beans called gigantes; canned butter beans are a practical substitute.

content.text: Gigantes plaki is a dish that rewards patience. The beans go into the oven in a fairly loose tomato sauce and come out an hour later in something altogether richer, the oil has emulsified into the sauce, the beans have absorbed the flavours, and the whole thing has thickened to a consistency that coats every bean. It is served warm rather than hot, with good bread and sometimes a piece of feta alongside.

Dried gigantes beans, soaked overnight and boiled until just tender, are the traditional ingredient. Tinned butter beans are a practical substitute that saves a day of planning. Either works; the flavour difference is smaller than the effort difference.

items.name: Butter beans
items.prepNote: tinned, drained and rinsed, two 400 g tins

items.name: Extra-virgin olive oil
items.prepNote: use good oil. It matters here

content.text: A deep ovenproof casserole or Dutch oven is ideal. The dish needs walls tall enough to hold the sauce as it bubbles.

Heat the oven to 160°C fan. Warm half the olive oil in an ovenproof casserole over medium heat. Cook {{onion}} finely chopped onion, {{celery}} chopped celery stalks, and {{carrot}} chopped carrot for 10 minutes until soft. Add {{garlic}} of sliced garlic and {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée; cook for 2 minutes. Pour in the hand-crushed {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper, and stir to combine.

Add {{butter-beans}} of drained butter beans to the sauce and stir gently to coat. Pour the remaining olive oil over the top. Transfer to the oven, uncovered, and bake for 45 to 60 minutes. Check after 30 minutes: if the sauce looks very dry, add a splash of water. By the end, the sauce should be thick and the oil integrated, with the beans showing a little colour on top. Stir in the dill and parsley and taste for seasoning. Serve warm, not piping hot.

items.fix: Tinned beans need only to heat through and absorb flavour. Keep the oven at 160°C fan and check at 45 minutes.
items.cause: Tinned beans overcooked before adding, or baked at too high a temperature.

items.fix: Add 100 ml of water and continue baking. A little pooled liquid at the 30-minute mark is normal.
items.cause: Oven running hot, or the casserole too wide and shallow.
items.symptom: Sauce too dry midway through baking

items.fix: Stir gently just before serving. The olive oil should have emulsified into the sauce by the end of the bake.
items.cause: Sauce not stirred enough before serving, or olive oil added all at once rather than poured over the top to baste.

items.fix: Taste after baking and adjust. This dish depends on a generous hand with salt and good olive oil, cheap oil makes a noticeably blander result.

content.text: Gigantes plaki appears on the Greek meze table with olives, bread, and dips. It also works as a main dish with a chunk of feta and a glass of retsina. This is classic taverna food, served at room temperature near the harbour at lunchtime. The name means baked giants. It refers to the large, flat white beans called gigantes. They have a creaminess when cooked low and long that smaller beans cannot match.
